segments.s595-g25: ازین صد به پنجاه بازآمدند is a compressed poetic order: ازین (از + این, “of these”) marks the subset, پنجاه is the target number, and بازآمدند is a past plural verb “they returned/ came back.” به here has the sense of movement/endpoint “to/toward” (they were reduced/returned to fifty). The whole phrase therefore states that of the hundred, fifty came back.
segments.s595-g26: پر از چاره و پرنیاز آمدند is two adjectival phrases + past plural verb: پرِ از چاره “full of chāra (devices/solutions/stratagems)” and پرنیاز (a compound adjective “full of need/entreaty”). آمدند is the same past plural verb “they came.” The construction uses پر + از + noun to mean “full of X.”
segments.s595-g27: ز پنجاه بهرام بود از نخست literally places the partitive z پنجاه (“of the fifty”) first. بود is the past copula “was,” and از نخست here is a predicative phrase meaning “from the first/among the first.” Word order is topicalized (partitive fronted) followed by subject (بهرام) and predicate.
segments.s595-g28: اگر introduces a conditional (“if”). جست is past of جستن meaning “sought” here. پای پدرگر is a genitive phrase: پای = “foot” used idiomatically for support/protection, پدرگر (compound) = “fatherly/step‑father or father-figure.” نجست is the negative past (ن + جست) meaning “he did not seek/find.” Together the clause says that even if he sought his father(’s support), he did not find it.
segments.s595-g29: ز پنجاه بازآوریدند سی: ز پنجاه is the poetic partitive “from the fifty.” بازآوریدند is a past plural transitive verb “they brought back / they restored,” and the postposed numeral سی is the object “thirty.” Poetic word order places the partitive and verb before the numeral object.
segments.s595-g30: ز ایرانی و رومی و پارسی enumerates the origin/identity of those thirty: the preposition/partitive ز here means “of/from,” followed by three nouns linked with و. Each noun names a people (ایرانی, رومی, پارسی) and functions as a noun phrase apposition to the thirty.
segments.s595-g31: ز سی نیز is the partitive/topic “of the thirty, too.” بهرام is the subject. بد is an adjective/adverb here modifying the following compound پیش‌رو (“in front/foremost”); together بد پیش رو means roughly “notably/ill‑or well‑fashioned in front” but in context functions idiomatically as “took the lead / stood out at the front.” Word order places the topic first and the descriptive predicate after the name.
segments.s595-g32: که introduces an explanatory clause “for/that.” هم … و هم is the correlative pair “both … and …”; تاجور بود is the predicate “was a tajūr (merchant/trader),” and شیر نو is a nominal phrase “a new/young lion” (شیر = lion, نو = new/young). The clause gives the reason why Bahram stood out.
segments.s595-g17: تو از ما یکی باش is an imperative instruction: تو (you) + از ما یکی (one/of us) + باش (imperative of بودن) = “be one of us.” The conjunction و links a second imperative: شاهی گزین where شاهی (kingship / a king) is the object and گزین is the 2nd‑person singular imperative “choose.”
segments.s595-g18: که introduces a result/relative clause. خوانند is a 3rd‑person plural present/formal narrative form meaning “they call/cry out.” هرکس is an indefinite subject “whoever/anyone.” برو آفرین is an exclamatory pair: برو (imperative ‘go’) plus آفرین (an acclamation ‘bravo’), here reported as what people cry when someone is proclaimed.
segments.s595-g19: سه روز is a time expression “three days.” اندران is a compact prepositional/locative element meaning “among them / in that” and links to کار (the affair); شد is the past of شدن ‘to become/happen,’ and روزگار here names the period/time, so the clause means literally ‘a time (روزگار) became (شد) as an affair among them’ = ‘the affair lasted three days.’
segments.s595-g20: که introduces the content/result clause: جویند is a 3rd‑person plural present/narrative form from جستن/جویدن meaning “they search/seeking”; ز ایران is the source prepositional phrase “from Iran”; and یکی شهریار is the object phrase “a single king” (یکی = one/a certain; شهریار = king/ruler).
segments.s595-g21: نوشتند is past 3rd‑person plural “they wrote.” پس is an adverb “then/after.” The sequence نام صد نامور groups the noun and its modifiers: نام (name) + صد (a hundred) + نامور (renowned) — i.e. ‘the names of a hundred famed ones.’ The verb precedes its objects, a common poetic word order.
segments.s595-g22: فروزنده is an adjectival/epithetic participle meaning “illuminating/brightening” and applies to the following nouns: تاج، تخت و کمر — ‘crown, throne, and girdle’ (a triad of royal insignia). The adjective precedes the nouns in this poetic listing.
segments.s595-g23: ازان صد (from/of those hundred) is a genitive/partitive phrase. یکی نام بهرام بود = ‘one (یکی) — the name (نام) was Bahram (بهرام) — بود is the past copula ‘was,’ so the clause states that one of the hundred bore the name Bahram.’
segments.s595-g24: که begins a relative clause modifying بهرام: در پادشاهی دلارام بود = ‘he was in/within the kingdom/kingship of Dolaram.’ در is the locative preposition ‘in,’ پادشاهی = ‘kingdom/kingship,’ دلارام is the place or proper name, and بود is the past copula ‘was.’
segments.s595-g33: ز + numeral (سی) marks the source/starting number: “from the thirty.” کرد is the simple past of کردن ‘to make/do’ and here governs the following noun phrase داننده موبد as agent (the knowing mobed) who performs the action. Word order is somewhat inverted for poetic effect: literal order = “From thirty (ز سی) made the knowing mobed (کرد داننده موبد) four.”
segments.s595-g34: This continues the object/result of کرد: چار is the numeral “four”; وزین is an epithet applied to چار meaning “weighty/grave/important.” The second part names one member of those four: بهرام بد شهریار = “Bahram, the bad king,” where بد is an epithet ‘bad/ill’ and شهریار is the noun ‘king.’
segments.s595-g35: چو is the temporal conjunction “when.” تنگ here describes a state (“narrow/strained”), and اندرآمد literally ‘entered/came in’ is used idiomatically to mean “became” or “came upon” (a change of condition). ز شاهی سخن = “the speech/discussion about kingship” (ز = about/of, شاهی = kingship, سخن = speech/discussion).
segments.s595-g36: ز ایرانیان = ‘of/from the Iranians’ (z = from/about). هرک او is an older form meaning ‘whoever he (was)’ (هرک = whoever, او = he). بد کهن are two adjectives applied to that referent: کهن ‘old’ and بد here ‘ill’ or ‘bad’ (contextually ‘old or ill’). The clause is elliptical and continues the narrative in the following lines.
segments.s595-g37: گفتند is the past plural ‘they said’ introducing reported speech. Inside the quotation نخواهیم is a negative future/optative first‑person‑plural form ‘we will not (want to / we will not)’, so the structure is: They said (گفتند), “We will not ...”. بهرام را is the definite object (را marks the direct object/topic) of the following verb phrase in the quotation.
segments.s595-g38: This is the object-complement list of the quotation: a string of predicates/epithets applied to بهرام. دلیر = ‘brave’; سبکسار = ‘light‑headed’ or ‘frivolous’ here used adjectivally; خودکام = ‘self‑willed/autocratic’. The final را reiterates the object marking for emphasis (poetic repetition).
segments.s595-g39: خروشی is a substantive meaning ‘a roar/clamour/commotion.’ برآمد is the past tense ‘rose/issued forth.’ میان سران = ‘among the chiefs/leaders’ (میان = among, سران = leaders). Typical SOV/adjacent order in poetry places the verb after the subject-noun.
segments.s595-g40: دل هرکسی = ‘the heart of each person/everyone’; تیز گشت is a two-word predicate meaning ‘became keen/quick/eager’ (تیز = keen, گشت = became). اندران = ‘therein/among them’ (adverbial locative). The clause describes the emotional reaction of the assembled people.
segments.s595-g41: چنین is an adverb meaning “thus/so” and precedes the verb to modify the manner of speaking. گفت is the past tense (3rd‑person) of گفتن “to say” (verb–subject order is common in epic Persian), منذر is the subject (a proper name) that follows the verb, and به ایرانیان is a prepositional phrase “to the Iranians” marking the indirect object/recipient.
segments.s595-g42: The initial که introduces reported content (‘that’). خواهم is first‑person present/optative meaning “I wish / I want” used in reported speech; the second که introduces the subordinate clause with دانم (here present/subjunctive sense ‘to know’ — desire that one know). به سود و زیان is a prepositional phrase meaning “with regard to profit and loss” (به + noun phrase).
segments.s595-g43: کزین is the archaic contracted preposition (کز + این) meaning “from this”; سال is the noun “year.” ناخورده is an adjectival past participle placed before the noun and here conveys the sense “untried / inexperienced.” شاه جوان is the noun phrase “the young king,” with جوان as a postnominal or appositive adjective in poetry.
segments.s595-g44: چرایید is a past tense verb meaning literally “he grazed / pastured” and in poetic usage can mean wandered or tended (here attributive to the king’s activity). پر before nouns works as an intensifier meaning “full (of),” so پر درد means “full of pain.” تیره‌روان is a compound adjective (tireh‑ravan) meaning “dark‑souled / ill‑fated,” connected by و to درد.
segments.s595-g45: بزرگان is the plural subject “the great ones / elders.” به پاسخ is a prepositional phrase literally “with/for an answer” and modifies the verb. بیاراستند is the past plural of the archaic verb بیاراستن (بیا‑راستن) meaning “they arranged/composed/embellished,” so the clause means “the elders prepared an answer.”
segments.s595-g46: بسی here functions as an adverb/intensifier modifying the following phrase (classical use 'very/much'); خسته‌دل is a compound adjective/noun meaning “weary‑hearted” describing the desired tone. پارسی indicates the language or style “in Persian,” and خواستند is the past plural “they requested/asked for.” The whole segment means they requested a very sorrowful reply in Persian.
segments.s595-g47: ز is the poetic form of از meaning “from”: ز ایران = “from Iran.” کرا is an archaic relative pronoun (“who/whom”) introducing a relative clause; it links to the following adjective خسته meaning “weary/tired.” بد here functions as an intensifying/predicative particle meaning “ill/bad” or “badly,” and یزدگرد is the proper name Yezdgerd — together the sequence forms an embedded relative clause describing persons from Iran affected by Yezdgerd.
segments.s595-g48: یکایک is an adverb meaning “one by one / one and another” and precedes the locative phrase بران دشت (بران = بر آن “on that/thereon,” and دشت = “plain”). کردند is the past plural ‘they did / made’ and, with گرد (here functioning adverbially as “around / about”), yields the sense “they went about/they encircled that plain.”
segments.s595-g57: غمی گشت is a nominal predicate + copula: literally "a grief became" (i.e. he became grieved); گشت is the past of گشتن used as 'became'. زان کار is a prepositional phrase 'from that deed' giving the cause. The proper name بهرام follows and سخت is an adverbial/postpositive modifier meaning 'severely/very' — word order is poetic (predicate + cause + subject + manner).
segments.s595-g58: به خاک پدر is a dative/goal phrase 'to (the) father's dust' (i.e. to his father's grave). گفت here is the past of گفتن 'said' introducing speech; کای is the vocative particle 'O' and شوربخت is the addressee 'unfortunate/wretched one.' Note the poet places the vocative after گفت rather than before the quotation.
segments.s595-g59: اگر introduces a conditional 'if'. چشم شادیت is a construct in which شادیت ('your joy/joyfulness') qualifies چشم ('eye') — idiomatically 'your joyous eye' or 'your loving/joyful gaze'. بر دوختی literally reads 'you sewed on' (دوختی is past of دوختن), but here the verb is used idiomatically: 'to fix one's gaze on' someone.
segments.s595-g60: روان را is the definite object 'the spirit/soul' marked by را. به آتش سوختی is a verbal phrase 'burned into/with fire' (به marks the instrument/target here) and چرا introduces the question 'why'. سوختی is the simple past 'you burned' (rhetorical second-person).
segments.s595-g61: جهانجوی is an epithet 'world-seeking' or 'ambitious', modifying the proper name منذر. به بهرام گفت is 'said to Bahram' (به marks the indirect object). Again, گفت is the past of 'to say' introducing the reported clause.
segments.s595-g62: که introduces reported speech 'that...'. این بد is 'this bad/evil (thing)'. بریشان is an archaic fused preposition + pronoun (بر ایشان = 'to/for them' or 'from their knowledge') meaning roughly 'from/for them' in this context. نباید is the impersonal modal 'must not/should not' and نهفت is the infinitive/past-stem 'to hide/conceal' so the clause means 'this bad thing should not be hidden from them.'
segments.s595-g63: سخنها شنیدی is a past clause 'you heard the words' (سخنها plural 'words/speeches', شنیدی second-person past). تو پاسخ‌گزار is an address/apposition 'you, responder' — the vocative or epithet placed after the clause.
segments.s595-g64: که introduces the content: 'that...'. تندی here means 'harshness/fierceness' (an abrasive tone), نه خوب آید is a negative existential/impersonal construction 'does not suit/come well' (آید 'comes, befits'), and از شهریار literally 'from a king' = 'for a king' (از used to indicate source/role). The whole clause: 'that harshness does not become a king.'
segments.s595-g49: بریده is a past participle/adjectival form 'cut off' placed before its object (poetic inversion). یکی را marks ‘one (person)’ as the definite direct object with را; the following list دو دست و دو پای names the parts that were cut off. The normal prose order would be something like «دو دست و دو پای یکی را بریده» but Ferdowsi places the participle first for rhythm.
segments.s595-g50: یکی 'one (another person)' is the subject/agent reference here; مانده is the past participle/verb 'remained/was left'. بر جای is a prepositional phrase 'on the spot/there'. و links the clause to the following short clause; جانش = جان + ـش 'his life' and به جای repeats the idea 'in place' meaning his life remained (i.e. he was still alive). Note the elliptical, compact poetic phrasing.
segments.s595-g51: یکی را marks ‘one (man)’ as the definite object. The coordinated nouns دو دست و دو گوش و زبان list the body parts; in Persian the object marker را often appears before such a coordinated list. The verb/participle that completes the idea follows (in the next segment).
segments.s595-g52: بریده‌شده is a compound past participle 'having been cut off' describing the listed parts / the person; چون = 'like/as' introduces a simile; تن 'body' and بی‌روان 'without soul' = 'lifeless' close the simile. The participle modifies the preceding noun phrase, producing the sense '...cut off, like a lifeless body.'
segments.s595-g53: یکی را marks one man as the object; ز تن دور کرده literally 'having removed from the body' (ز = 'from', دور here = 'removed/away', کرده is the participle/auxiliary 'made/done') and دو کفت names what was removed (کفت = the palms). The verb cluster is compact and inverted for poetic effect.
segments.s595-g54: ازان 'of those/from those' + مردمان 'people' sets the group; ماند is the past 'remained/was left'. منذر is a proper name (Munḏir) placed after the verb (poetic word order). شگفت here qualifies his state — 'astonished'/'struck with wonder' — so the clause means 'of those people, Munḏir remained, amazed.'
segments.s595-g55: یکی را marks the affected man as object. به مسمار uses به + instrument (here 'with/by a stake/nail') — in classical/poetic usage به can mark the instrument. کنده (past participle of کندن) 'torn out/dug out' follows, and دو چشم names the removed parts; word order is inverted for emphasis (the instrument and participle precede the explicit body-part phrase).
segments.s595-g56: چو here = 'when'. منذر is the subject (Munḏir). بدید is the past 'saw'. آن = 'that' (referring to the deed just described). برآورد in this construction means 'brought forth/raised' (i.e. he produced/was filled with) and خشم 'anger' names what he raised — together: 'when Munḏir saw that, he was filled with/rose in anger.'
segments.s595-g1: چنین گفت is a compact, poetic word order: چون + این = 'thus' (adverb) followed by گفت (past tense 'said'), so literally 'thus said Bahram.' بهرام is the proper name (subject) placed after the verb for rhetorical effect. کای here is the vocative particle 'O' addressing the following noun (مهتران).
segments.s595-g2: This is a noun phrase modifying the addressee: جهاندیده and سالخورده are adjectives placed before the head noun سران ('leaders/chiefs'). The coordinating و links the two adjectives; سران is the plural head (the people being addressed).
segments.s595-g3: پدر بر پدر is a poetic repetition meaning 'from father to father' or 'from ancestor to ancestor' (a genitival/lineage idea); پادشاهی is the noun 'kingship/kingdom.' مراست is an archaic fused form (مرا است) functioning as a copula meaning 'is mine / belongs to me' — in classical Persian the clitic construction fuses as one word.
segments.s595-g4: چرا is the interrogative 'why' (here rhetorical). بخشش is a noun 'pardon/grant' (context determines whether 'pardon' or 'gift'); اکنون 'now' adverbially modifies the clause. برای شما is 'for you' (برای + pronoun) and ست (shortened 'است') is the copula 'is' — together 'is for you.' The clause asks why mercy/forgiveness would now be for you.
segments.s595-g5: به آواز is a prepositional/adverbial phrase meaning 'with a voice / aloud' (به + آواز). گفتند is the past plural 'they said' (گفت + -ند), and ایرانیان is the subject 'the Iranians' — the verb precedes the subject here, a common poetic inversion emphasizing the act of speaking.
segments.s595-g6: که introduces the reported (object) clause. ما را is 'us' with the definite/object marker را (marks the direct object). شکیبا is an adjective 'patient' used here as an object complement ('make us patient'). مکن is the negative imperative 'do not (make)'. بر زیان is a prepositional phrase meaning 'toward/against loss' (بر + زیان) that modifies the state 'patient' (i.e. 'patient in the face of loss').
segments.s595-g7: نخواهیم is first‑person plural negative/future of خواستن ('we will not / we do not want'). یکسر is an adverb 'altogether/entirely.' به شاهی ترا is a predicate structure: به شاهی = 'to kingship / as king' and ترا is the clitic 'you' (ت + را) used as the direct object; together the clause means 'we will not (entirely) make you king' (or 'we will not grant you complete kingship').
segments.s595-g8: بر و بوم is a compound noun 'land and home / homeland.' ما را is 'our' / 'of us' with را marking the definite phrase. سپاهی ترا is 'your army/warriors' formed from سپاهی (army/warrior) plus the 2nd‑person enclitic ت (your) and a final را (definite/object marker). Together the clause offers an alternative role: 'be (or remain) the army/protector of our land.'
segments.s595-g9: کزین is the prepositional phrase 'from this' (کز + این). تخمه here means 'offspring/line' and is the object of کزین. پرداغ و دودیم و درد are three coordinated predicates describing the (implicit) subject; دودیم carries the first-person plural ending -یم so that the whole coordinated string is understood as predicative of 'we' (a common poetic device: suffix on the final item governs the series).
segments.s595-g10: شب و روز is the fixed phrase 'night and day'. با introduces the instrument/condition 'with', here linking to the causes of affliction. پیچش و باد سرد is a coordinated noun phrase: پیچش (here 'convulsion/writhing') and باد modified by سرد ('cold wind').
segments.s595-g11: چنین گفت بهرام places the verb گفت ('said') before the speaker (بهرام) — a common poetic word order equivalent to 'بهرام چنین گفت'. کری is a predicate noun 'insensibility/deafness' and رواست is روا + ست (archaic contraction of است) meaning 'is permissible/fitting'; the copula is attached to روا.
segments.s595-g12: هوا is the subject meaning 'desire/passion'. بر is the preposition 'upon/on', governing دل ('heart'), and هرکسی 'every person' modifies دل (on the heart of every person). پادشاست is a nominal predicate ('is king/sovereign') with the copula fused into the form.
segments.s595-g13: مرا‌گر is a conditional cluster where مرا (me) appears with گر ('if'); the phrase functions like 'if (it be) about me / if you do not want me'. نخواهید is the second-person (plural/formal) present negative 'you do not want/wish' in classical form. بی‌رای من means 'without my vote/consent' (بی- 'without' + رأی 'vote/opinion'), modifying the condition.
segments.s595-g14: چرا is 'why' introducing a rhetorical question. کس is 'someone/anyone'; نشانید is an archaic verbal form meaning 'to seat/appoint/settle' (here 'would set/seat'); بر جای من is a prepositional phrase 'upon my place' = 'in my stead'. The verb is used to ask why anyone would place another in the speaker's position.
segments.s595-g15: چنین گفت موبد again places the verb 'said' before the speaker (موبد, the Zoroastrian priest). که introduces a reported clause. از راه داد is a genitival phrase 'by/from the path of justice' (از + راه 'path' + داد 'justice').
segments.s595-g16: نه ... نه is the correlative 'neither ... nor ...'. خسرو here denotes 'king' in a general sense. گریزد is the verb 'flees/escapes' and governs both coordinated subjects; the second conjunct (کهتر نژاد) omits the verb by ellipsis (understood to share گریزد). کهتر نژاد means 'of inferior lineage / the low-born'.

sentences.s595-s13: Of those hundred, fifty returned; they came back full of schemes and full of supplication.
sentences.s595-s14: Of the fifty, Bahram was one from the first; if he sought his father's support, he did not find it.
sentences.s595-s15: From the fifty they brought back thirty — thirty were Iranian, Roman, and Persian.
sentences.s595-s16: Of the thirty, too, Bahram took the lead, for he was both a merchant and a young lion.
sentences.s595-s9: Join us and be one of us, and choose a king, so that whenever anyone is proclaimed they will cry out “Bravo — go!”
sentences.s595-s10: For three days the affair lasted, a span of time in which they sought from Iran a single king.
sentences.s595-s11: They then wrote down the names of a hundred renowned men — illuminators of crown, throne, and girdle.
sentences.s595-s12: Of those hundred, one was named Bahram, who was in the kingship/realm of Dolaram.
sentences.s595-s17: From the thirty the learned mobed made four; among those weighty four was Bahram, the bad king.
sentences.s595-s18: When the discussion of kingship grew constrained, among the Iranians whoever was old or ill...
sentences.s595-s19: They said, “We will not call Bahram brave, light‑headed, and autocratic.”
sentences.s595-s20: A clamor rose among the chiefs; every man’s heart there grew sharp/eager.
sentences.s595-s21: Munẓir thus said to the Iranians that he wished to know what would be for gain and what for loss.
sentences.s595-s22: From that year the inexperienced young king wandered/pastured, full of pain and ill‑fated.
sentences.s595-s23: The elders composed an answer; they asked for a deeply sorrowful reply in Persian.
sentences.s595-s24: From Iran, those whom Yezdgerd had wearied, one by one they went upon that plain and circled it.
sentences.s595-s29: Because of that deed Bahram was deeply grieved; he said to his father's dust, 'O wretched one.'
sentences.s595-s30: If you had fixed your joyous eye, why did you burn the spirit in the fire?
sentences.s595-s31: The world-seeking Munẓir said to Bahram that this bad thing must not be concealed from them.
sentences.s595-s32: You have heard the words, O responder; harshness does not become a king.
sentences.s595-s25: They cut off one man’s two hands and two feet; another remained on the spot, his life still in place (i.e. still alive).
sentences.s595-s26: They cut off a man’s two hands, two ears and tongue; he lay like a lifeless body.
sentences.s595-s27: He tore two palms from one man’s body; of those people Munḏir alone remained, astonished.
sentences.s595-s28: They tore out one man’s two eyes with a stake; when Munḏir saw that, he burst into anger.
sentences.s595-s1: Thus Bahram said, "O chiefs, worldly‑wise and aged leaders."
sentences.s595-s2: Kingship, from father to father, belongs to me — why, then, should mercy now be for you?
sentences.s595-s3: The Iranians said aloud, 'Do not make us patient in the face of loss.'
sentences.s595-s4: We will not entirely make you king; be the army (protector) of our land.
sentences.s595-s5: From this stock we are sore and smoked and afflicted, night and day, with convulsions and a cold wind.
sentences.s595-s6: Thus Bahram said: 'Insensibility is permissible; desire rules over every person's heart.'
sentences.s595-s7: If you do not want me — without my vote — why should anyone seat someone in my place?
sentences.s595-s8: Thus the mobed said that, by the path of justice, neither king nor low-born can escape.
